turns.content: Good morning. I have a practical question. I enjoy reading poetry and mathematics, but I have been finding it increasingly difficult to read for extended periods. The text becomes unclear at the edges of the page. I am wondering whether you could recommend strategies for reading with impaired peripheral vision, or perhaps alternative formats that might be easier.

Good morning. I'm sorry to hear about the difficulty — reading is clearly important to you, so let's see what we can do.

Peripheral vision loss affecting reading is a specific challenge. Could you tell me a bit more about what's happening with your vision? Have you had it evaluated by an eye doctor? Knowing the underlying cause would help me suggest the most appropriate strategies.

Yes, I have glaucoma. The open-angle type, which is progressive. I use latanoprost drops every morning and evening to control the intraocular pressure. My ophthalmologist measured it at 18 millimeters of mercury at my last appointment — she considers that stable and within an acceptable range. But the visual field continues to narrow, even with the pressure well managed. I think this is simply the nature of the condition. The damage already done to the optic nerve does not reverse itself.

You're exactly right about that. Open-angle glaucoma is managed, not cured — latanoprost is a first-line treatment, and 18 mmHg is a good target. But even with well-controlled pressure, the peripheral field can continue to narrow because existing nerve damage doesn't regenerate.

For reading specifically, peripheral vision loss creates several challenges: difficulty tracking across a full line of text, losing your place when moving to the next line, and fatigue from the extra concentration required.

Here are strategies that help:

**Physical adjustments:**
- Use a reading guide or ruler under each line to aid tracking
- Choose larger print editions — fewer words per line means less peripheral scanning
- Use a reading stand to maintain consistent distance
- Strong, direct task lighting on the page (a desk lamp, not overhead light)

**Digital options:**
- E-readers like Kindle Paperwhite let you adjust font size freely. For someone with narrowing peripheral fields, this may be the single biggest improvement — you control exactly how much text fits within your reliable central vision.
- For go diagrams specifically, a tablet lets you zoom into specific board areas

What kind of reading material do you work with most? That will help me prioritize the suggestions.

All three of my interests present difficulties, though in different ways. Poetry collections are typically set in small type. Mathematics I do recreationally now — number theory problems — and I can write those out in my own hand at whatever size suits me. But the go books are the most challenging, because board diagrams require scanning across a grid of intersections. That demands exactly the peripheral awareness I am losing.

Each of those needs a slightly different approach:

**Poetry:** An e-reader would transform this for you. E-ink screens look like paper, not like a phone screen — no glare, gentle front-lighting, and you can set the font to whatever size keeps the text within your comfortable field. Korean poetry collections are increasingly available digitally.

**Mathematics:** You've already found the best solution — working in your own handwriting at your own scale. A desktop magnifier with a wide lens can help when you do need to reference a published text.

**Go diagrams:** This is the hardest case because the whole board matters at once. Two practical options:
- Play through book diagrams on your physical goban rather than trying to read them on the page. Transfer the position stone by stone. This is slower but eliminates the peripheral scanning problem entirely.
- Use go software on a tablet where you can zoom into specific regions.

I'd also add one important adaptation for your board play itself: have you noticed any issues seeing stones at the edges of the board during actual games?

I have, yes. I sometimes miss stones placed on the first and second lines, particularly in corners opposite to where my attention is focused. I think this is the peripheral loss at work. It is an odd experience — like solving an equation with a variable you did not know was there. The information exists on the board, but it falls outside my window of reliable perception.

That's a very precise description of the problem, and the equation metaphor is apt — a hidden variable changes everything. Here are some practical adaptations for board play:

**Develop a systematic scanning habit:**
- After every move (yours and your opponent's), deliberately scan all four corners and edges with your central vision
- Make it a ritual, like checking boundary conditions in a proof
- This replaces the automatic peripheral awareness that's fading with deliberate, systematic checking

**Physical adjustments:**
- Sit closer to the board to reduce the visual angle — more of the board falls within your remaining field
- In club games, it's perfectly acceptable to verbally confirm placement: "You played at 3-4 in the upper right?" This isn't weakness; even professional players confirm moves.

**For teaching:**
- Use smaller demonstration areas (9x9 or partial board positions) when possible
- This is actually better pedagogy for beginners anyway — isolated positions are clearer

The scanning habit is the most important one. You're replacing an automatic system with a deliberate one. It takes practice, but once habitual, it becomes nearly automatic again.

The scanning habit is excellent advice. It reminds me of a principle in mathematical proof — one must verify not only the main argument but the boundary cases. The edges of the board are, quite literally, boundary cases. I have been neglecting them because my vision once handled them without effort. Now I must be deliberate about what was previously automatic. I think I will adopt this immediately.

That's a perfect parallel — and honestly, deliberately scanning the full board after every move may actually improve your play beyond just compensating for the vision. Even sighted professional players miss edge plays when they get absorbed in a local fight. Your new habit might make you more aware of the whole position than you were before the glaucoma.

One last thought: please keep up diligently with the latanoprost schedule and your ophthalmology appointments. Maintaining that pressure at 18 mmHg or lower is the single most important thing you can do to slow further field loss. Every degree of peripheral vision you preserve now is worth protecting.

I am very consistent with the drops. Morning and evening, without exception. I think of it as maintaining a boundary condition — if I neglect it, the pressure rises and the domain of my vision contracts further. The mathematics of it is quite unforgiving. Thank you for this conversation. It has been practical and clear, which I appreciate. I will speak to my son about an e-reader when he visits next. Good morning.

Your discipline with the drops is exactly right — maintaining the boundary condition to keep the solution valid. And your son will probably be delighted to help with the e-reader; that's the kind of practical project that engineers enjoy.

Good morning, and I hope the scanning habit serves you well at the board. Enjoy the reading — whether on paper or eventually on screen.
